front.title: AI and Consciousness
front.subtitle: Exploring the boundary between algorithmic processing and subjective experience.

back.text:
The question of whether a machine can possess consciousness is a central theme in both computer science and philosophy. In technical terms, this often involves the distinction between weak AI, which simulates intelligence to perform tasks, and strong AI, which would theoretically possess a mind and self-awareness. While modern large language models can mimic human conversation with high fidelity, they operate through statistical probability rather than internal experience or sentience.

To evaluate machine consciousness, researchers often look to the Turing Test or the Integrated Information Theory. The former focuses on external behavior, while the latter attempts to quantify the complexity of internal data integration. As AI systems become more sophisticated, the debate shifts from what a machine can do to what a machine might feel, challenging our legal and ethical frameworks regarding the rights and treatment of synthetic entities.
